text: fat is 0.3g. the fat quality is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, clean nutrient density is the real test.
carbs is 3.3g. fibre is where things start to fall off. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
protein is 3.1g. protein quality is a real strength here. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
vitamin a at 0% DV, vitamin c at 4% DV, vitamin k at 0% DV stand out most. that is barely moving the needle.
minerals are basically not part of the case here.

the upsides first. very low downside whole food because vegetables win a lot simply by offering clean nutritional payoff. easy to build meals around because a practical plus because good foods need realistic use cases. good volume for the calories because that matters for satiety and general usefulness.

on the weaker side. energy density is very low because good for some use cases, but not enough on its own. usually needs pairing to make a full meal because most vegetables are not complete by themselves. freshness and prep can matter because practical friction is still a real downside.
D tier. Best only when you need a low-commitment add-on rather than a nutrient-dense anchor.
